description: Eiken Grade 2 vocabulary list with 2000 words for ENG Quest

exampleSentences: Mobile banking offers the convenience of transferring money without visiting a branch.

exampleSentences: The landlord agreed to furnish the studio with a bed and desk before the tenant moved in.

exampleSentences: He used his gap year to intern at a wildlife sanctuary in Kenya before starting university.

words.reading: コーワーキングスペース

exampleSentences: Economic hardship drove emigration from the island, leaving many villages half-empty.

exampleSentences: The doctor examined her tonsils and found they were severely swollen from repeated infections.

words.englishDefinition: a hormone released under stress that increases heart rate and prepares the body for action

exampleSentences: Regular exercise is believed to raise serotonin levels, which may help stabilise mood over time.

words.reading: カイロプラクティック

words.englishDefinition: a hormone produced by the brain that regulates the sleep-wake cycle in response to darkness

words.reading: プリベンティブメディシン
exampleSentences: Vaccination campaigns are a cornerstone of preventive medicine, stopping disease before it spreads.

words.englishDefinition: a branch of medicine focused on averting illness through screening, lifestyle guidance, and immunisation

words.reading: インターミテントファスティング

exampleSentences: After fracturing his tibia, he was referred to orthopedics for surgical evaluation.

words.reading: エコーカーディオグラム

exampleSentences: Winning the national debate championship elated her far more than any individual grade ever had.

words.jpTranslation: 恥ずかしい思いをさせる

exampleSentences: The contradictory findings in the two studies perplexed researchers who had expected consistent results.

words.reading: サイコロジカルウェルビーイング

exampleSentences: Her therapist used cognitive behavioral therapy to help her identify the automatic thoughts fuelling her depression.

exampleSentences: Her obsession with symmetry made it impossible for her to leave the office until every item was perfectly aligned.

exampleSentences: The basement remained damp throughout winter, encouraging the growth of mould on the lower walls.

exampleSentences: Construction workers took mandatory breaks in the shade during the sweltering August afternoons.

exampleSentences: She took a brisk walk along the riverbank each morning to clear her head before the workday.

exampleSentences: Torrential rain overwhelmed the drainage system, leaving several city streets completely impassable.

exampleSentences: The storm surge accompanying the typhoon pushed seawater several kilometres inland, submerging low-lying neighbourhoods.

exampleSentences: The earthquake triggered a tidal wave that struck the coastline with little warning, devastating port towns.

exampleSentences: Crops withered across the parched farmland after six consecutive weeks without any rainfall.

words.reading: コーラルブリーチング
exampleSentences: Scientists documented widespread coral bleaching across the Great Barrier Reef last summer.

exampleSentences: The manager gave verbal approval before the team submitted the formal budget request.

exampleSentences: Crossed arms and averted eyes are nonverbal signals that often indicate discomfort.

exampleSentences: The ministry convened a roundtable of economists to advise on the new fiscal policy.

exampleSentences: The facilitator used small-group exercises to draw out quieter participants.

exampleSentences: The professor posed a hypothetical scenario: what would you do if the budget were cut by half?

exampleSentences: When she asked 'Can we really afford to ignore this?', it was a rhetorical question designed to persuade.

words.reading: パネルディスカッション

exampleSentences: The chairperson called for a vote after both sides had presented their arguments.

exampleSentences: He noticed her leaning forward and mirroring her posture helped establish an instant connection.

words.englishDefinition: belonging to the informal language used in everyday conversation rather than formal writing

words.reading: パブリックスピーキング

exampleSentences: The Nobel laureate delivered a keynote address that set the tone for the entire symposium.

exampleSentences: The poet described grief as a stone in her chest, a powerful simile that resonated with readers.

words.englishDefinition: a figure of speech that compares two things using 'like' or 'as'

exampleSentences: Critics argued the new surveillance law infringed on citizens' civil liberties.

exampleSentences: His prior conviction for fraud made it harder to find employment after his release.

words.englishDefinition: a formal declaration by a court that someone is guilty of a criminal offence

exampleSentences: Workers joined the trade union to collectively bargain for higher wages and safer conditions.

exampleSentences: The algorithm is proprietary, so competitors cannot legally replicate the firm's core technology.

exampleSentences: Every function in the payment module must pass its unit test before being merged into the main codebase.

words.reading: コンティニュアスインテグレーション

exampleSentences: The team rebuilt the image-processing feature as a serverless function, eliminating the need to maintain dedicated servers.

words.reading: ニューラルネットワーク

words.reading: ナチュラルランゲージプロセッシング

exampleSentences: The engineering team deployed a hotfix within two hours after users reported being locked out of their accounts.

words.reading: フリップドクラスルーム
exampleSentences: In a flipped classroom model, students watch video lectures before class and use lesson time for problem-solving.
Research suggests the flipped classroom increases student engagement because class time focuses on active discussion.
words.englishDefinition: an instructional approach where students study new content at home and practise applying it in class

words.reading: プロジェクトベースドラーニング
exampleSentences: Project-based learning allowed students to design a community garden, applying science and maths to a real problem.
Teachers who use project-based learning report higher student motivation than those using traditional lectures alone.
words.englishDefinition: a teaching method in which students gain knowledge by working on a real or meaningful project over an extended period

words.reading: ディファレンシエーション
exampleSentences: Effective differentiation means assigning advanced readers more complex texts while supporting struggling learners with scaffolding.
The school's differentiation strategy groups students flexibly so that each child is appropriately challenged.
words.englishDefinition: the practice of adapting teaching methods, materials, or tasks to meet individual learners' varying needs and abilities

words.reading: ダイアグノスティックテスト
exampleSentences: The teacher gave a diagnostic test on the first day to identify which grammar points needed the most attention.
A diagnostic test at the start of term revealed that many students lacked foundational fraction skills.
words.englishDefinition: an assessment used at the beginning of instruction to identify a learner's existing knowledge and specific gaps

exampleSentences: Peer tutoring pairs stronger students with those who are struggling, reinforcing knowledge for both parties.
Studies show that peer tutoring in mathematics significantly raises attainment in low-income secondary schools.
words.englishDefinition: a learning arrangement in which students teach or assist each other rather than relying solely on the teacher

words.reading: フォーマティブフィードバック
words.jpTranslation: 形成的フィードバック
exampleSentences: The teacher provided formative feedback on each draft so students could revise before the final submission.

distractors: コードとしてのインフラ
words.englishDefinition: ongoing feedback given during the learning process to guide improvement before final evaluation

words.reading: サマティブアセスメント
exampleSentences: The final examination serves as a summative assessment of everything taught throughout the academic year.

words.englishDefinition: an evaluation conducted at the end of an instructional period to measure what students have learned overall

words.reading: ポートフォリオアセスメント
exampleSentences: Portfolio assessment captures growth over time by reviewing a student's best and most recent work together.
Art students preferred portfolio assessment because a single exam could not reflect months of creative development.
words.englishDefinition: an evaluation method that judges a learner's progress by examining a collected body of their work over time

words.reading: ギフテッドエデュケーション
exampleSentences: Gifted education programmes often allow students to accelerate through the curriculum at their own pace.
Critics argue that gifted education can deepen inequality if selection processes disadvantage lower-income students.
words.englishDefinition: specialised instruction designed to meet the academic needs of students who show exceptionally high ability

words.reading: コラボラティブラーニング
exampleSentences: Collaborative learning tasks require students to negotiate ideas and divide responsibilities, mirroring real workplace dynamics.
The professor restructured lectures into collaborative learning sessions where small groups solved case studies together.
words.englishDefinition: an educational approach in which students work together on tasks to construct knowledge and solve problems jointly

words.reading: ゾーンオブプロクシマルデベロップメント
exampleSentences: A skilled tutor identifies each student's zone of proximal development and sets tasks just beyond current ability.
Vygotsky's concept of the zone of proximal development emphasises that learning advances through guided interaction.
words.englishDefinition: the range of tasks a learner can perform with guidance but not yet independently, where learning is most effective

words.reading: インデペンデントスタディ
exampleSentences: She chose independent study in her final semester, designing her own research project on urban biodiversity.
Independent study requires strong self-discipline, since there are no fixed class times to structure your week.
words.englishDefinition: a course or period of learning in which a student pursues a self-directed topic without regular class instruction

exampleSentences: The hedge fund shorted bank stocks just before the financial crisis and made enormous profits.
Unlike mutual funds, a hedge fund can use leverage and short-selling to bet against markets.
words.englishDefinition: a privately managed investment fund that uses high-risk strategies such as short-selling and leverage to seek high returns

words.reading: イニシャルパブリックオファリング
exampleSentences: The tech startup's initial public offering was oversubscribed within hours of the shares going on sale.
Before an initial public offering, companies must disclose detailed financial information to regulatory authorities.
words.englishDefinition: the first time a private company sells its shares to the public on a stock exchange

words.reading: アカウンツレシーバブル

Investors check accounts receivable closely to assess whether a company's reported revenue will actually be collected.
words.englishDefinition: money that customers owe a business for goods or services already delivered but not yet paid for

words.reading: アカウンツペイアブル
exampleSentences: Managing accounts payable efficiently lets businesses hold cash longer without damaging supplier relationships.
The audit revealed that accounts payable had been understated, making the company appear more profitable than it was.
words.englishDefinition: money a business owes to its suppliers or creditors for goods or services already received but not yet paid

words.reading: ワーキングキャピタル
exampleSentences: The startup burned through its working capital faster than expected and needed an emergency loan to pay staff.
Strong working capital gives a business the flexibility to accept large orders without straining cash flow.

exampleSentences: Even when sales dropped to zero, the factory faced a fixed cost of fifty thousand dollars per month in rent and insurance.
Entrepreneurs must cover fixed costs before they reach break-even, regardless of how many units they sell.
words.englishDefinition: a business expense that remains constant regardless of the level of goods or services produced

exampleSentences: Raw materials are a variable cost because each additional unit produced requires more inputs.
The bakery's variable cost per loaf rose sharply when wheat prices spiked on global markets.
distractors: シビルパートナーシップ
words.englishDefinition: a business expense that changes in proportion to the quantity of goods or services produced

exampleSentences: Years of austerity left hospitals understaffed and roads in disrepair across the region.
The government defended its austerity programme, arguing that reducing the deficit was unavoidable after the crisis.
words.englishDefinition: a set of government policies that cut public spending and reduce the budget deficit, often during an economic crisis

words.reading: クォンティタティブイージング
exampleSentences: The central bank launched a round of quantitative easing, purchasing government bonds to lower long-term interest rates.

words.englishDefinition: a monetary policy in which a central bank buys financial assets to increase the money supply and stimulate the economy

words.reading: エコノミックスティミュラス
exampleSentences: The trillion-dollar economic stimulus package included direct payments to households and loans for small businesses.
Economists debated whether the economic stimulus should target infrastructure or immediate consumer spending.
words.englishDefinition: government spending or tax cuts designed to boost economic activity during a slowdown or recession

exampleSentences: Interest payments on the national debt consumed nearly fifteen percent of the government's annual budget.
The opposition warned that emergency spending would push the national debt to an unsustainable level.
words.englishDefinition: the total amount of money a government owes to its creditors, accumulated through years of borrowing

exampleSentences: During the credit crunch, banks refused to lend even to financially healthy businesses, paralyzing the economy.
The credit crunch of 2008 forced governments worldwide to bail out failing financial institutions.
words.englishDefinition: a sudden sharp reduction in the availability of loans from banks, usually causing economic hardship

words.reading: プログレッシブタックス
exampleSentences: Under a progressive tax system, a millionaire pays a higher percentage of income than a minimum-wage worker.
Supporters argue that a progressive tax reduces inequality without discouraging moderate-income earners.
words.englishDefinition: a tax system in which the rate increases as the taxable amount increases, so higher earners pay a larger share

words.reading: バリューアデッドタックス
exampleSentences: Many European countries charge a value added tax of twenty percent on most consumer goods and services.
Small businesses must register to collect value added tax once their annual revenue exceeds the legal threshold.
words.englishDefinition: a consumption tax added at each stage of production and ultimately paid by the end consumer

words.reading: マーケットイクイリブリアム
exampleSentences: At market equilibrium, the quantity of concert tickets sellers offer exactly matches the quantity buyers want.
A sudden drought disrupted the agricultural market equilibrium, pushing vegetable prices sharply higher.
words.englishDefinition: the point at which the quantity of a good supplied equals the quantity demanded, producing a stable price

words.reading: プライスエラスティシティ
exampleSentences: Luxury goods typically have high price elasticity because consumers can easily choose not to buy them when prices rise.
Economists measure price elasticity to predict how much a tax increase will reduce cigarette consumption.
words.englishDefinition: a measure of how much the quantity demanded or supplied of a good changes in response to a price change

words.reading: ガバメントインターベンション
exampleSentences: Government intervention in the housing market through rent controls often leads to unintended shortages.
Without government intervention, monopolies can charge excessive prices and stifle competition.
words.englishDefinition: actions taken by a government to influence or regulate economic activity, markets, or social outcomes

words.reading: ソブリンウェルスファンド
exampleSentences: Norway's sovereign wealth fund holds stakes in thousands of global companies.
The Gulf state used its sovereign wealth fund to invest in European infrastructure.
words.englishDefinition: a state-owned investment fund that manages a country's accumulated surplus revenues

exampleSentences: Auditors confirmed the firm's solvency before approving the merger.
Maintaining solvency during a cash-flow crisis requires careful debt management.
words.englishDefinition: the ability of a person or organization to pay all debts when they are due

words.reading: クラウドファンディング
words.jpTranslation: クラウドファンディング
exampleSentences: The documentary was financed entirely through crowdfunding from 4,000 supporters.
Crowdfunding platforms let small businesses raise capital without approaching banks.
words.englishDefinition: raising money for a project by collecting small contributions from many people, typically online

words.reading: ミューチュアルファンド
exampleSentences: She moved her savings into a low-cost index mutual fund for retirement.
A mutual fund spreads risk across dozens of stocks, reducing exposure to any single company.
words.englishDefinition: an investment vehicle that pools money from many investors to buy a diversified portfolio of securities

exampleSentences: The bank required real estate as collateral before approving the business loan.
Without adequate collateral, the startup struggled to secure financing.
words.englishDefinition: an asset pledged to a lender as security for repayment of a loan

exampleSentences: The central bank injected liquidity into markets to prevent a credit freeze.
Treasury bonds are popular precisely because of their high liquidity.
words.englishDefinition: the ease with which an asset can be converted into cash without significantly affecting its price

words.reading: アモーティゼーション
exampleSentences: The accountant showed how amortization of the patent reduces taxable income each year.
Monthly mortgage payments include both interest and amortization of the principal.
words.englishDefinition: the gradual paying off of a debt or the systematic write-down of an asset's cost over time

exampleSentences: Founders typically retain majority equity until a major funding round dilutes their stake.
She built up equity in her home over ten years of mortgage payments.
words.englishDefinition: the value of ownership interest in a company or property, representing the residual after debts are paid

exampleSentences: The rival's aggressive pricing eroded their market share by eight percentage points.
Gaining market share in Southeast Asia is central to the company's five-year strategy.
words.englishDefinition: the portion of total sales in a market that a particular company or product captures

words.reading: トレードデフィシット
exampleSentences: A widening trade deficit prompted lawmakers to debate new import restrictions.
The country's trade deficit narrowed as export volumes rose in the third quarter.
words.englishDefinition: the amount by which a country's imports exceed its exports over a given period

words.reading: インポートデューティ
exampleSentences: Raising import duty on steel was intended to protect domestic manufacturers.
The retailer's pricing reflected the steep import duty applied to foreign electronics.
words.englishDefinition: a tax levied by a government on goods brought into the country from abroad

exampleSentences: After deducting all expenses and taxes, the chain reported net profit of $2.3 million.
Investors focus on net profit because it reflects what the company actually earns.
words.englishDefinition: the amount of money a company retains after all costs, taxes, and expenses have been subtracted from revenue

words.reading: オペレーティングコスト
exampleSentences: Automating the warehouse cut operating costs by a third within eighteen months.
Rising energy prices pushed operating costs above the budgeted level.
words.englishDefinition: the day-to-day expenses a business incurs to keep its core operations running

words.reading: ブレークイーブンポイント
exampleSentences: The café calculated its breakeven point at 80 covers per day.

words.englishDefinition: the level of output or revenue at which total costs exactly equal total income, resulting in neither profit nor loss

words.reading: マーケットキャピタリゼーション
exampleSentences: Apple's market capitalization briefly surpassed three trillion dollars last year.
Analysts classify companies by market capitalization as small-, mid-, or large-cap.
words.englishDefinition: the total market value of a company's outstanding shares, calculated by multiplying share price by share count

words.reading: バランスオブペイメンツ
exampleSentences: A persistent deficit in the balance of payments can weaken a nation's currency.
The IMF reviewed the country's balance of payments before approving emergency lending.
words.englishDefinition: a record of all financial transactions between a country and the rest of the world over a set period

exampleSentences: High-frequency traders use arbitrage to profit from millisecond price gaps across exchanges.

words.englishDefinition: the simultaneous buying and selling of an asset in different markets to profit from a price difference

exampleSentences: The government imposed a price ceiling on rental housing to protect low-income tenants.
Critics argued the price ceiling on fuel led to shortages at petrol stations.
words.englishDefinition: a legally set maximum price that sellers are allowed to charge for a good or service

exampleSentences: The EU's price floor on wine protects vineyards from being undercut by cheap imports.

words.englishDefinition: a legally set minimum price below which a good or service cannot be sold

words.reading: コンシューマーコンフィデンス
exampleSentences: Consumer confidence rebounded after unemployment figures dropped for the third month running.

words.englishDefinition: a measure of how optimistic consumers feel about the economy and their personal financial situation

exampleSentences: The government's fiscal year runs from April to March, unlike the calendar year.

words.englishDefinition: a twelve-month accounting period used by a government or company that may differ from the calendar year

exampleSentences: The fund reports returns on a calendar year basis, from January through December.

words.englishDefinition: the twelve-month period beginning on 1 January and ending on 31 December

words.reading: ターンアラウンドタイム
exampleSentences: The lab reduced its turnaround time for test results from five days to two.
Customers chose the printer because of its fast turnaround time on large orders.
words.englishDefinition: the time required to complete a process or task from start to delivery

exampleSentences: Due to supply chain delays, the lead time for semiconductors stretched to six months.
Building in adequate lead time before the product launch prevents last-minute shortages.
words.englishDefinition: the amount of time between placing an order and receiving or completing the goods or service

exampleSentences: The trading platform displays real-time price changes down to the second.
Surgeons can now monitor a patient's vital signs in real-time during remote procedures.
words.englishDefinition: occurring or processed immediately as events happen, without any perceptible delay

exampleSentences: The university held an open day for prospective students considering the engineering program.
A prospective study tracked the same cohort for twenty years to measure health outcomes.
words.englishDefinition: expected or likely to happen in the future, or relating to a potential future situation

exampleSentences: The passport was rejected at the border because it was within six months of expiry.

words.englishDefinition: the point at which something reaches the end of its valid period and is no longer usable

exampleSentences: Construction is a cyclical industry that booms when interest rates are low.
The analyst warned that some of the profit gains were cyclical rather than structural.
words.englishDefinition: recurring in regular cycles, especially rising and falling in line with economic conditions

exampleSentences: The editorial board holds biweekly meetings to review submissions and plan upcoming issues.

words.englishDefinition: occurring every two weeks

exampleSentences: With hindsight, expanding into that market during a recession was a costly mistake.

words.englishDefinition: understanding of an event or decision that one gains only after it has occurred

exampleSentences: His foresight in stockpiling components shielded the factory from the supply-chain crisis.

exampleSentences: Chronic tardiness disrupted the morning briefings and frustrated the entire team.
The manager addressed the employee's tardiness directly rather than letting it slide.
words.englishDefinition: the habit or fact of arriving late or not completing something on time

exampleSentences: The urgency in the director's voice signaled that the deadline could not be extended.
Climate scientists stress the urgency of reducing emissions in the next decade.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of requiring immediate attention or action

exampleSentences: Patient safety is the hospital's top priority and overrides cost considerations.
She listed her priorities for the quarter so the team could allocate resources accordingly.
words.englishDefinition: something considered more important than other things and therefore dealt with first

exampleSentences: Research shows that multitasking during lectures significantly impairs retention of the material.

words.englishDefinition: the practice of handling more than one task at the same time

exampleSentences: The judge gave greater weight to contemporaneous notes written on the day of the incident.

words.englishDefinition: existing or occurring at the same time as something else

exampleSentences: The prolific composer wrote over 600 pieces before his fortieth birthday.

words.englishDefinition: producing a large quantity of work, results, or offspring

exampleSentences: He updated his insurance policy to name his partner as the sole beneficiary.

words.englishDefinition: a person or group that receives money, property, or other advantages from something

exampleSentences: Both unions accepted a neutral mediator to help bridge the wage dispute.

exampleSentences: The contract required both companies to submit unresolved disputes to a certified arbitrator.

exampleSentences: The elderly professor became a surrogate mentor to students without academic role models.

words.englishDefinition: acting as a substitute for another person or thing

exampleSentences: He confided in his therapist as a professional confidant rather than burdening his family.

words.englishDefinition: a person trusted with one's private thoughts or personal matters

exampleSentences: The defense attorney and prosecutor are legal adversaries but respect each other professionally.

words.englishDefinition: a person or group that opposes or competes against another

exampleSentences: Surveillance footage identified an accomplice who had unlocked the back door for the thieves.

exampleSentences: A disagreement over company direction created a lasting rift between the two co-founders.

words.englishDefinition: a serious break or rupture in a relationship or agreement

exampleSentences: The dissolution of the thirty-year partnership left both firms scrambling for new alliances.

exampleSentences: The historian traced the royal lineage back through twelve generations of documented records.

exampleSentences: Online genealogy databases have made it possible to discover relatives across continents.

words.englishDefinition: the study or tracing of a person's or family's line of descent and ancestral history

exampleSentences: Without the patronage of wealthy merchants, Renaissance artists could rarely afford materials.

words.englishDefinition: financial or other support given by a patron to an artist, institution, or cause

exampleSentences: The study found that cohabitation before marriage does not necessarily predict divorce.

words.reading: コーディペンデンシー

exampleSentences: The counselor explained how gaslighting gradually convinces victims that their memory is faulty.

exampleSentences: Learning to say no at work is a key part of maintaining healthy boundaries.

words.reading: シビルパートナーシップ
words.jpTranslation: シビルパートナーシップ
exampleSentences: The couple registered a civil partnership to gain the same legal protections as married spouses.

words.englishDefinition: a legally recognized union between two people that confers rights similar to marriage

words.reading: ロングディスタンスリレーションシップ

words.reading: アンコンディショナルラブ
exampleSentences: Unconditional love means accepting someone fully, even when they disappoint you.

words.reading: エモーショナルアビュース
exampleSentences: Hotline counselors are trained to recognize emotional abuse even when no physical harm has occurred.

exampleSentences: His narcissism made it difficult to give colleagues credit for shared achievements.

exampleSentences: She recognized the manipulation when he reframed every argument to make her feel responsible.

words.reading: ノンバイオレントコミュニケーション

exampleSentences: Couples therapy often teaches nonviolent communication to reduce heated arguments.

exampleSentences: Offering validation after someone shares a difficulty can strengthen trust in a relationship.

words.englishDefinition: acknowledgment that a person's feelings or experiences are understandable and legitimate

exampleSentences: Her mentor's affirmation that she was capable helped her push through self-doubt.

words.reading: コンフリクトレゾリューション
exampleSentences: Conflict resolution training helped the department reduce workplace disputes significantly.

exampleSentences: The feuding neighbors eventually agreed to share the disputed fence costs.

words.englishDefinition: engaged in a prolonged, bitter quarrel or rivalry with another person or group

words.reading: コンスティテューショナル
exampleSentences: The court ruled that the new surveillance law violated constitutional privacy rights.

exampleSentences: His secondment to the Tokyo branch gave him valuable international experience.

words.englishDefinition: a temporary transfer of an employee to another department or organization

exampleSentences: In a hierarchical organization, decisions flow from senior executives down to front-line staff.

exampleSentences: Employees laid off in the restructuring received two weeks of severance for each year served.

words.englishDefinition: compensation paid to an employee whose job is terminated by the employer

exampleSentences: Exporting luxury goods to Asia has proven lucrative for many European brands.

exampleSentences: Students must pass entrance exams before they can matriculate at the graduate school.

exampleSentences: The training manual presents tasks in sequential order so new hires can follow each step.

exampleSentences: A momentary loss of concentration caused him to miss the key data on the slide.

exampleSentences: The forthcoming quarterly report is expected to show a significant rise in revenue.

exampleSentences: He offered a belated apology two weeks after the argument had already damaged the relationship.

exampleSentences: The magistrate fined the driver for reckless behavior in a school zone.

words.reading: コンスティテュエンシー
exampleSentences: The senator held town halls to hear directly from her constituency about housing concerns.

words.reading: ビュロークラティック

words.reading: ラティフィケーション
exampleSentences: The climate agreement gained full effect only after ratification by fifty member nations.

words.englishDefinition: the formal approval of an agreement or treaty by an authorized body

exampleSentences: As a longtime advocate for refugee rights, she lobbied parliament for stronger protections.

words.englishDefinition: a person who publicly supports a cause, or to actively support such a cause

exampleSentences: The city council voted to allocate additional funding to public transport infrastructure.
